name: Fee Simple Absolute
description: The most complete form of ownership recognised in English law. The holder possesses the full bundle of Honore incidents with no temporal or conditional limitations.
notes: The baseline against which all lesser estates are measured. Nearest approximation to absolute dominion.

name: Fee Simple Defeasible
description: Ownership subject to a condition subsequent or determinable limitation. The estate may be cut short upon the occurrence of a specified event.
subtypes: Fee simple subject to condition subsequent
Fee simple subject to executory limitation
notes: Capital and alienation are constrained because the defeasibility clouds the holder's ability to dispose freely.

name: Life Estate
description: An estate measured by the duration of a natural life. The tenant has full possessory rights but must not commit waste, as the property will pass to the remainderman.
notes: The doctrine of waste constrains capital rights. Alienation is limited to the remaining life interest — the transferee gets no more than the original life tenant's duration.

name: Leasehold
description: A possessory estate for a fixed term or periodic tenancy. The lessee has exclusive possession but is bound by the lease covenants and the landlord's reversionary interest.
notes: Alienation depends on assignment/subletting covenants. Capital rights are minimal — the tenant cannot destroy the landlord's reversion.

name: Licence
description: A bare permission to enter or use land. Does not create a proprietary interest and is generally revocable at will, though contractual and estoppel licences may be irrevocable.
key_authority: Thomas v Sorrell (1673); Ashburn Anstalt v Arnold [1989]
notes: The critical distinction from a lease is the absence of exclusive possession. A licence binds only the parties and does not run with the land.

name: Easement
description: A right to use another's land for a specific purpose (e.g., right of way, light, drainage). Requires a dominant and servient tenement and must accommodate the dominant land.
notes: Must satisfy the Ellenborough Park criteria. Cannot amount to exclusive possession of the servient land or a right to take the natural produce.

name: Restrictive Covenant
description: A negative obligation restricting the use of land, enforceable in equity against successors in title. The covenant must touch and concern the dominant land.
notes: A purely negative right — the holder cannot do anything on the burdened land but can prevent the burdened owner from specified acts. Positive covenants do not run in equity.

name: Mortgage
description: A security interest in land. The mortgagee holds a charge over the property entitling them to possession and sale upon default, but the mortgagor retains the equity of redemption.
key_authority: LPA 1925, s.85-87; Four-Maids Ltd v Dudley Marshall [1957]
notes: Before default, most incidents remain with the mortgagor. Upon default, the mortgagee's capital and income rights activate dramatically. Ranges reflect the dormant-to-active spectrum.

name: Trust (Beneficial Interest)
description: The equitable interest of a beneficiary under a trust. Legal title vests in the trustee, but the beneficiary holds the economic substance — income, capital expectations, and beneficial enjoyment.
notes: Management is delegated to the trustee. Under Saunders v Vautier, an absolutely entitled adult beneficiary can collapse the trust and recover full incidents.

name: Adverse Possession
description: Possessory title acquired through continuous, open, and adverse occupation for the limitation period. The squatter's rights crystallise progressively against the paper owner.
key_authority: Limitation Act 1980, s.15; JA Pye v Graham [2003]
notes: Possession is paramount — the squatter must demonstrate factual possession and intention to possess. Full alienation rights require registration under the Land Registration Act 2002.

name: Profit à Prendre
description: A right to take something from another's land — natural produce, soil, minerals, fish, or game. Unlike an easement, it involves removal of part of the servient land's substance.
notes: Distinguished from easement by the right of severance — taking part of the land or its produce. Can exist in gross (without a dominant tenement) unlike easements.
